[SBS](/wiki/Silver_Bauhinia_Star "Silver Bauhinia Star") (born 18 May 1955), previously known as **Donald Chow**,[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a Hong Kong actor and filmmaker.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) In a [film career](/wiki/Chow_Yun-fat_filmography "Chow Yun-fat filmography") spanning more than forty-five years, Chow has appeared in over 100 television drama series and films.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Known for his versatility, encompassing action and melodrama, comedy and historical drama, his accolades include three [Hong Kong Film Awards](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Film_Awards "Hong Kong Film Awards") for [Best Actor](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Film_Award_for_Best_Actor "Hong Kong Film Award for Best Actor") and two [Golden Horse Awards](/wiki/Golden_Horse_Film_Festival_and_Awards "Golden Horse Film Festival and Awards") for [Best Actor](/wiki/Golden_Horse_Award_for_Best_Leading_Actor "Golden Horse Award for Best Leading Actor").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

**Chow Yun-fat** [SBS](/wiki/Silver_Bauhinia_Star "Silver Bauhinia Star") (born 18 May 1955), previously known as **Donald Chow**,[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a Hong Kong actor and filmmaker.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) In a [film career](/wiki/Chow_Yun-fat_filmography "Chow Yun-fat filmography") spanning more than forty-five years, Chow has appeared in over 100 television drama series and films.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Known for his versatility, encompassing action and melodrama, comedy and historical drama, his accolades include three [Hong Kong Film Awards](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Film_Awards "Hong Kong Film Awards") for [Best Actor](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Film_Award_for_Best_Actor "Hong Kong Film Award for Best Actor") and two [Golden Horse Awards](/wiki/Golden_Horse_Film_Festival_and_Awards "Golden Horse Film Festival and Awards") for [Best Actor](/wiki/Golden_Horse_Award_for_Best_Leading_Actor "Golden Horse Award for Best Leading Actor").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Chow was propelled to fame by [TVB](/wiki/TVB "TVB") dramas such as *[The Good, The Bad And The Ugly](/wiki/The_Good,_the_Bad_and_the_Ugly_(TV_series) "The Good, the Bad and the Ugly (TV series)")* (1979) and *[The Bund](/wiki/The_Bund_(TV_series) "The Bund (TV series)")* (1980). His first acclaimed film was the Hong Kong political drama *[The Story of Woo Viet](/wiki/The_Story_of_Woo_Viet "The Story of Woo Viet")* (1981), in which he played a Vietnamese refugee struggling to reach the United States.[[5]](#cite_note-5) He is known for his collaborations with filmmaker [John Woo](/wiki/John_Woo "John Woo") in five [Hong Kong action](/wiki/Hong_Kong_action_cinema "Hong Kong action cinema") films: *[A Better Tomorrow](/wiki/A_Better_Tomorrow "A Better Tomorrow")* (1986), which made Chow a box-office superstar in Asia,[[6]](#cite_note-6) *[A Better Tomorrow II](/wiki/A_Better_Tomorrow_II "A Better Tomorrow II")* (1987), *[The Killer](/wiki/The_Killer_(1989_film) "The Killer (1989 film)")* (1989), *[Once a Thief](/wiki/Once_a_Thief_(1991_film) "Once a Thief (1991 film)")* (1991), and *[Hard Boiled](/wiki/Hard_Boiled "Hard Boiled")* (1992). He also starred in the video game *[Stranglehold](/wiki/Stranglehold_(video_game) "Stranglehold (video game)")* (2007), produced by Woo. Chow also made several popular action films with Hong Kong director [Ringo Lam](/wiki/Ringo_Lam "Ringo Lam"), including *[City on Fire](/wiki/City_on_Fire_(1987_film) "City on Fire (1987 film)")* (1987), *[Prison on Fire](/wiki/Prison_on_Fire "Prison on Fire")* (1987), and *[Full Contact](/wiki/Full_Contact_(1992_film) "Full Contact (1992 film)")* (1992). Chow is credited for bringing [Hong Kong gangster films](/wiki/Heroic_bloodshed "Heroic bloodshed") to world prominence.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

His other notable Hong Kong and Chinese films include *[An Autumn's Tale](/wiki/An_Autumn%27s_Tale "An Autumn's Tale")* (1987), *[God of Gamblers](/wiki/God_of_Gamblers "God of Gamblers")* (1989), *[All About Ah-Long](/wiki/All_About_Ah-Long "All About Ah-Long")* (1989), *[Curse of the Golden Flower](/wiki/Curse_of_the_Golden_Flower "Curse of the Golden Flower")* (2006), *[Let the Bullets Fly](/wiki/Let_the_Bullets_Fly "Let the Bullets Fly")* (2010), *[From Vegas to Macau](/wiki/From_Vegas_to_Macau "From Vegas to Macau")* (2014), and *[Project Gutenberg](/wiki/Project_Gutenberg_(film) "Project Gutenberg (film)")* (2018). Chow made his [Hollywood](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States") debut in *[The Replacement Killers](/wiki/The_Replacement_Killers "The Replacement Killers")* (1998). He is also known in the West for *[The Corruptor](/wiki/The_Corruptor "The Corruptor")* (1999), *[Anna and the King](/wiki/Anna_and_the_King "Anna and the King")* (1999), *[Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon](/wiki/Crouching_Tiger,_Hidden_Dragon "Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon")* (2000), *[Bulletproof Monk](/wiki/Bulletproof_Monk "Bulletproof Monk")* (2003), and *[Pirates of the Caribbean: At World's End](/wiki/Pirates_of_the_Caribbean:_At_World%27s_End "Pirates of the Caribbean: At World's End")* (2007).

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chow_Yun-fat&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Chow was born in [Lamma Island](/wiki/Lamma_Island "Lamma Island"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong"), to Chow Yung-wan, who worked on a [Shell Oil Company](/wiki/Shell_Oil_Company "Shell Oil Company") tanker, and Chan Lai-fong, who was a cleaning lady and vegetable farmer.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) Chow grew up in a farming community on Lamma Island, in a house with no electricity.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) He woke up at dawn each morning to help his mother sell [herbal jelly](/wiki/Grass_jelly "Grass jelly") and [Hakka tea-pudding](/wiki/Hakka_tea-pudding "Hakka tea-pudding") on the streets; in the afternoons, he went to work in the fields. His family moved to [Kowloon](/wiki/Kowloon "Kowloon") when he was ten. At 17, Chow left school to help support the family by doing odd jobs including a bellboy,[[14]](#cite_note-14) postman, camera salesman, and taxi driver.

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chow_Yun-fat&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

In 1973, the 18-year-old Chow responded to a newspaper advertisement for [TVB](/wiki/TVB "TVB")'s actor training program. After a one-year training, he signed a three-year contract with the TV station and made his acting debut in soap operas. He gained recognition in such dramas as *The Killer* (1976) and *Hotel* (1976). He had his breakout role in *[The Good, The Bad And The Ugly](/wiki/The_Good,_the_Bad_and_the_Ugly_(TV_series) "The Good, the Bad and the Ugly (TV series)")* (1979), followed by *[The Bund](/wiki/The_Bund_(TV_series) "The Bund (TV series)"),* a series about the gangsters in 1930s [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"). The latter made Chow a star across Asia.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Chow_Yun_Fat_2.JPG/250px-Chow_Yun_Fat_2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Chow_Yun_Fat_2.JPG)

Chow at the premiere of *Pirates of the Caribbean: At World's End* in 2007

Although Chow continued his TV success, his goal was to become a film actor. He made his film debut in 1976 after signing an exclusive contract with Goldig Films, then the third largest film company in Hong Kong.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Goldig Films, founded and solely-funded by Indonesian Chinese businessman Gouw Hiap Kian,[[16]](#cite_note-16) produced and distributed over 100 movies from 1972 to 1982.[[17]](#cite_note-17) However, Chow's occasional ventures into low-budget films in the 1980s after ones by Goldig were disastrous.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Most of Chow's movies with Goldig Films in the 1970s achieved high gross revenues of over HK$1 million per movie, which is a better box office performance than his movies in early 1980s, such as *Modern Heroes* (江湖檔案), *Soul Ash* (灰靈), [*The Bund* (上海灘)](/wiki/The_Bund_(TV_series) "The Bund (TV series)"), *The Bund Part 2* (上海灘續集).[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Success came when he teamed up with film director [John Woo](/wiki/John_Woo "John Woo") in the 1986 gangster action-melodrama *[A Better Tomorrow](/wiki/A_Better_Tomorrow "A Better Tomorrow")*, which swept the box offices in Asia and established Chow and Woo as megastars. *A Better Tomorrow* won him his first Best Actor award at the Hong Kong Film Awards. It was the highest-grossing film in Hong Kong history at the time, and set a new standard for Hong Kong gangster films. Taking the opportunity, Chow quit TV entirely. With his new image from *A Better Tomorrow*, he made many more '[gun fu](/wiki/Gun_fu "Gun fu")' or '[heroic bloodshed](/wiki/Heroic_bloodshed "Heroic bloodshed")' films, such as *[A Better Tomorrow II](/wiki/A_Better_Tomorrow_II "A Better Tomorrow II")* (1987), *[Prison on Fire](/wiki/Prison_on_Fire "Prison on Fire")* (1987), *[Prison on Fire II](/wiki/Prison_on_Fire_II "Prison on Fire II") (1991)*, *[The Killer](/wiki/The_Killer_(1989_film) "The Killer (1989 film)")* (1989), *[A Better Tomorrow 3](/wiki/A_Better_Tomorrow_3 "A Better Tomorrow 3")* (1990) and *[Hard Boiled](/wiki/Hard_Boiled "Hard Boiled")* (1992). He won his second Best Actor award for his role in the more realistic crime thriller *[City on Fire](/wiki/City_on_Fire_(1987_film) "City on Fire (1987 film)")* (1987), an inspiration for [Quentin Tarantino](/wiki/Quentin_Tarantino "Quentin Tarantino")'s *[Reservoir Dogs](/wiki/Reservoir_Dogs "Reservoir Dogs")*.[[20]](#cite_note-G-20)

Chow may be best known for playing honorable tough guys, whether cops or criminals, but he has also starred in comedies like *[Diary of a Big Man](/wiki/Diary_of_a_Big_Man "Diary of a Big Man")* (1988) and *[Now You See Love, Now You Don't](/wiki/Now_You_See_Love,_Now_You_Don%27t "Now You See Love, Now You Don't")* (1992) and romantic blockbusters such as *[Love in a Fallen City](/wiki/Love_in_a_Fallen_City_(film) "Love in a Fallen City (film)")* (1984) and *[An Autumn's Tale](/wiki/An_Autumn%27s_Tale "An Autumn's Tale")* (1987), for which he was named Best Actor at the [Golden Horse Awards](/wiki/Golden_Horse_Awards "Golden Horse Awards"). He brought together his disparate personae in the 1989 film *[God of Gamblers](/wiki/God_of_Gamblers "God of Gamblers")*, directed by the prolific [Wong Jing](/wiki/Wong_Jing "Wong Jing"), in which he was by turns a suave charmer, a broad comedian, and an action hero. The film surprised many, became immensely popular, broke Hong Kong's all-time box office record, and spawned a series of gambling films as well as several comic sequels starring [Andy Lau](/wiki/Andy_Lau "Andy Lau") and [Stephen Chow](/wiki/Stephen_Chow "Stephen Chow"). The often tough demeanour and youthful appearance of Chow's characters has earned him the nickname "Babyface Killer."[[21]](#cite_note-P-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/HK_%E9%81%8E%E6%B5%B7%E9%9A%A7%E9%81%93%E5%B7%B4%E5%A3%AB111%E7%B7%9A_Bus_route_111_view_Kln_City_%E6%BC%86%E5%92%B8%E9%81%93%E5%8C%97_Chatham_Road_North_November_2019_SS2_13.jpg/250px-HK_%E9%81%8E%E6%B5%B7%E9%9A%A7%E9%81%93%E5%B7%B4%E5%A3%AB111%E7%B7%9A_Bus_route_111_view_Kln_City_%E6%BC%86%E5%92%B8%E9%81%93%E5%8C%97_Chatham_Road_North_November_2019_SS2_13.jpg)](/wiki/File:HK_%E9%81%8E%E6%B5%B7%E9%9A%A7%E9%81%93%E5%B7%B4%E5%A3%AB111%E7%B7%9A_Bus_route_111_view_Kln_City_%E6%BC%86%E5%92%B8%E9%81%93%E5%8C%97_Chatham_Road_North_November_2019_SS2_13.jpg)

Advertisement featuring Chow in 2019

The *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* proclaimed Chow Yun-Fat "the coolest actor in the world".[[22]](#cite_note-22) In the mid '90s, Chow moved to [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles") in an ultimately unsuccessful attempt to duplicate his success in Asia. His first two films, *[The Replacement Killers](/wiki/The_Replacement_Killers "The Replacement Killers")* (1998) and *[The Corruptor](/wiki/The_Corruptor "The Corruptor")* (1999), were box office failures. In his next film *[Anna and the King](/wiki/Anna_and_the_King "Anna and the King")* (1999), Chow teamed up with [Jodie Foster](/wiki/Jodie_Foster "Jodie Foster"), but the film underperformed at the box office. Chow accepted the role of [Li Mu-bai](/wiki/Crouching_Tiger,_Hidden_Dragon "Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon") in the (2000) film *[Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon](/wiki/Crouching_Tiger,_Hidden_Dragon "Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon").* It became a winner at both the international box office and the [Oscars](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards"). In 2003, Chow came back to Hollywood and starred in *[Bulletproof Monk](/wiki/Bulletproof_Monk "Bulletproof Monk")*. In 2004, Chow made a surprise cameo in director [Dayyan Eng](/wiki/Dayyan_Eng "Dayyan Eng")'s Chinese rom-com favourite *[Waiting Alone](/wiki/Waiting_Alone "Waiting Alone")*; it was the first time he was in a mainland Chinese film.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In 2006, he teamed up with [Gong Li](/wiki/Gong_Li "Gong Li") and [Jay Chou](/wiki/Jay_Chou "Jay Chou") in the film *[Curse of the Golden Flower](/wiki/Curse_of_the_Golden_Flower "Curse of the Golden Flower"),* directed by [Zhang Yimou](/wiki/Zhang_Yimou "Zhang Yimou").

In 2007, Chow played the pirate captain [Sao Feng](/wiki/Sao_Feng "Sao Feng") in *[Pirates of the Caribbean: At World's End](/wiki/Pirates_of_the_Caribbean:_At_World%27s_End "Pirates of the Caribbean: At World's End")*. However, his part was omitted when the movie was shown in [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China"), where, according to Chinese unofficial sources, government censors felt that Chow's character "vilified and humiliated" Chinese people.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

From 2014 to 2016, Chow reunited with his *[God of Gamblers](/wiki/God_of_Gamblers "God of Gamblers")* director [Wong Jing](/wiki/Wong_Jing "Wong Jing") to make the *[From Vegas to Macau](/wiki/From_Vegas_to_Macau "From Vegas to Macau")* franchise.[[25]](#cite_note-25) For the part, he lost 13 kg within 10 months.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In 2018, he co-starred with [Aaron Kwok](/wiki/Aaron_Kwok "Aaron Kwok") in *[Project Gutenberg](/wiki/Project_Gutenberg_(film) "Project Gutenberg (film)"),* which earned him another [Best Actor](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Film_Award_for_Best_Actor "Hong Kong Film Award for Best Actor") nomination at the [38th Hong Kong Film Awards](/wiki/38th_Hong_Kong_Film_Awards "38th Hong Kong Film Awards").[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In 2023, he became the second Hong Kong actor, after [Tony Leung](/wiki/Tony_Leung_Chiu-wai "Tony Leung Chiu-wai"), to be named Asian Filmmaker of the Year at the 28th [Busan International Film Awards](/wiki/Busan_International_Film_Festival "Busan International Film Festival").[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chow_Yun-fat&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Chow married twice. In 1983, he married [Candice Yu](/wiki/Candice_Yu "Candice Yu"), an actress with [Asia Television](/wiki/Asia_Television "Asia Television"); the marriage only lasted nine months. In 1986, Chow married [Singaporean](/wiki/Singaporean "Singaporean") Jasmine Tan. After having a stillborn daughter in 1991, they decided not to have children.

Despite being famous for using martial arts moves on the screen, Chow, in a conversation with *[Metro](/wiki/Metro_Broadcast_Corporation "Metro Broadcast Corporation")*, revealed that he relied heavily on stunt coordinators and was not all as ‘athletic’ as it seemed, mentioning, “I am not like [Bruce Lee](/wiki/Bruce_Lee "Bruce Lee") or [Jackie Chan](/wiki/Jackie_Chan "Jackie Chan")”.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In 2018, Chow’s wife Jasmine Tan disclosed that Chow’s net worth was HK$ 5.6b, which was not verified by any third party. Chow said he would donate 99% of his wealth to charity.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Chow maintains a modest public image by frequenting food stalls and public transportation in Hong Kong.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Chow ran a half marathon in less than 2 hours 30 minutes in November 2023.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In October 2014, Chow voiced support for students in the [Umbrella Movement](/wiki/Umbrella_Movement "Umbrella Movement"), a civil rights movement for universal suffrage in Hong Kong.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)
